reason_thou:
1. Understanding the Issue:
The loop-vectorizer crashes on the provided IR with an assertion inside LoopVectorizationPlanner::computeBestVF():
  assert((BestFactor.Width == LegacyVF.Width || planContainsAdditionalSimplifications(...)) && " VPlan cost model and legacy cost model disagreed");
This assertion checks that either (a) the new VPlan-based cost model (BestFactor) selects the same VF as the legacy cost model (LegacyVF), or (b) the chosen VPlan contains extra simplifications not seen by the legacy cost model, which could justify a difference. In the reproducer, BestFactor selects VF=1 (scalar) while LegacyVF selects VF=4 (vector), and planContainsAdditionalSimplifications returns false, triggering the assertion.

2. Analyzing opt's Log:
The pass fails early; no transformations occur. In the debugger, locals show BestFactor is scalar (VF=1) and LegacyVF is 4. FirstPlan has only the scalar VF recorded (vectorFactors() size 1). This indicates the new cost model chose the scalar plan while the legacy model judged VF=4 optimal. The assertion failure exposes a mismatch between the two cost models without recognized simplifications.

3. Root Cause Analysis:
The new VPlan-based cost model and the legacy cost model can legitimately disagree when neither side has missed any simplifications (e.g., incomplete parity in cost accounting of predicated or scalarized instructions). The current code assumes that any disagreement must be explainable via detected simplifications in the selected VPlan; otherwise it asserts. In practice, transitional differences in the still-evolving VPlan cost model (e.g., cost estimation of predicated variable shifts and urem chains) can yield a different VF than the legacy model even when no simplifications are flagged, producing false-positive assertion failures and aborts. The assertion is too strict and prevents graceful fallback. A safe resolution is to reconcile the disagreement by deferring to the legacy VF when there are no simplifications; this maintains correctness (legacy model has historically driven decisions) while allowing continued progress of the pass. This turns the assertion from a hard failure into a recovery path, preserving existing behavior and avoiding crashes.

4. Proposed Edit Point(s):
File: llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/LoopVectorize.cpp Lines 7369-7384.
Replace the current assertion block with logic that:
  (a) Computes LegacyVF and CostCtx as before.
  (b) If the widths differ, precompute costs and test for simplifications.
  (c) If no simplifications are found, adopt LegacyVF (BestFactor = LegacyVF) instead of asserting.
  (d) Retain a reduced assertion to catch only unexpected internal inconsistencies (e.g., missing scalar cost).
This change respects the intent of validating the new cost model but avoids aborting for benign disagreements lacking extra simplifications.

5. Conclusion:
By converting the rigid assertion into a controlled reconciliation step, the loop-vectorizer will no longer crash on the reproducer. The pass will fall back to the legacy VF choice when the new cost model disagrees without simplifications, maintaining correctness and stability while allowing further refinement of the VPlan cost model.
